name: 863 Program & NSFC Launch
nameZh: 国家高技术研究发展计划(863计划)
agency: State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: State High-Tech Research and Development Plan (approved by Deng Xiaoping, 5 March 1986)
shortDescription: Deng Xiaoping's national high-tech R&D fund — China's first sustained state money for biotechnology.
impact: Bankrolled the labs that built Chinese biotech, including the 1% slice of the Human Genome Project that became BGI; backed 5,200+ R&D projects before folding into the unified program in 2016.
keyProvisions: Named biotech as a priority field, alongside space, IT, lasers, and energy
Top-down R&D fund run by MOST, with senior scientists picking the projects
Paired with NSFC, China's first peer-reviewed basic-science funder (Feb 1986)
Folded into the unified National Key R&D Program in 2016, ending the 863 brand

name: 973 Basic Research Program
nameZh: 国家重点基础研究发展计划
agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, via the State Science and Education Steering Group under the State Council
documentRef: National Plan on Key Basic Research and Development (approved 4 June 1997)
shortDescription: Beijing's first big-money basic-science program — multi-year grants for upstream research years from any payoff.
impact: Funded 456 projects and 451 lead investigators by 2008, building the academic bench Chinese biotech later hired from — including the labs that turned APL into the first curable adult leukemia.
keyProvisions: 132 projects in the 1998–2002 batch — concentrated funding, not spread thin
Eight priority areas, including 'Population and Health' (cancer, stem cells)
Five-year projects led by a single 'chief scientist' who controlled the budget
Folded into the unified National Key R&D Program in February 2016

name: Interim HGR Measures
nameZh: 人类遗传资源管理暂行办法
agency: General Office of the State Council (transmitting joint draft by Ministry of Science and Technology and Ministry of Public Health)
documentRef: Guobanfa [1998] No. 36
shortDescription: China's first rule requiring Beijing's sign-off before Chinese tissue, blood, or DNA samples could leave the country.
impact: Established that Chinese DNA belongs to China; dormant for a decade, then used 2015-2018 to penalize BGI and WuXi AppTec at least six times for unauthorized sample exports.
keyProvisions: Covered tissue, cells, blood, DNA, and derived data — not just physical samples
Required Beijing's prior approval to collect, store, sell, or export any sample
Set up the China Administration of HGR under MOST as licensing gatekeeper
Forced foreign researchers to work through a Chinese partner with shared IP

name: Major New Drug Creation Megaproject
nameZh: 重大新药创制科技重大专项
agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, National Health Commission, Ministry of Finance, and National Development and Reform Commission
documentRef: National Medium- and Long-Term Science & Technology Development Plan (2006–2020), Megaproject No. 10
shortDescription: A 12-year national grant program that paid Chinese drugmakers to invent original drugs, not copy them.
impact: Funneled ~23.3B yuan (~US$3.3B) into 3,000+ R&D projects and produced 44 'Class 1' drugs by 2020 — 8x what China managed in prior decades.
keyProvisions: Grants paid in 5-year tranches across the 11th, 12th, and 13th Five-Year Plans
Targeted 10 priority diseases including cancer, diabetes, and infectious disease
Funded ~15 shared drug-discovery platforms that small firms could rent
Co-funded late-stage trials for camrelizumab, sintilimab, and Betta's icotinib

name: SFDA Director Executed
nameZh: 郑筱萸案
agency: Beijing No. 1 Intermediate People's Court / State Food and Drug Administration
documentRef: Zheng Xiaoyu corruption verdict (sentenced 2007-05-29, executed 2007-07-10); follow-on Provisions for Drug Registration (SFDA Order No. 28, effective 2007-10-01)
shortDescription: China executed its top drug regulator Zheng Xiaoyu for taking US$850K in bribes to wave through unsafe medicines.
impact: Exposed that Zheng's agency had rubber-stamped ~150,000 drug approvals in eight years — including a tainted antibiotic that killed 14 — and set the stage for the 2015–2018 overhaul.
keyProvisions: Executed July 10, 2007 — highest-ranking Chinese official killed since 2000
Took bribes from 8 drug makers, 1997–2006; one made antibiotic that killed 14
Six approved drugs proven counterfeit; tainted cough syrup killed ~100 in Panama
Order 28 (Oct 2007): panel approvals, surprise inspections, ban on data fakers

name: Made in China 2025
nameZh: 中国制造2025
agency: State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: Guofa [2015] No. 28
shortDescription: A 10-year industrial plan naming biopharma and medical devices among 10 sectors China would build itself, not import.
impact: Locked biotech into Beijing's industrial frame and made it a US target — imports' share of China's medical-device market fell from 24% to 14% by 2023, and biopharma drew Section 301 tariffs in 2018.
keyProvisions: Named biomedicine and high-end medical devices a top-10 priority sector
Targeted 70% domestically made mid/high-end devices in county hospitals by 2025
Backed antibodies, ADCs, vaccines, and cell therapies as priority drug classes
Raised manufacturing R&D-to-revenue target to 1.68% by 2025, from 0.95% in 2015

name: Order 117 / 722 Self-Inspection
nameZh: 关于开展药物临床试验数据自查核查工作的公告（2015年第117号）
agency: China Food and Drug Administration
documentRef: CFDA Announcement No. 117 (2015) — known in Chinese as the '722 event' (七二二事件) for the date it was issued
shortDescription: Gave sponsors of 1,622 pending Chinese drug filings one month to re-audit their trial data — or admit it was faked.
impact: 1,165 of 1,622 applications were voluntarily withdrawn and 30 rejected — roughly 80% gone — collapsing the NDA backlog from ~22,000 in Sept 2015 to under 3,500 by end-2018.
keyProvisions: Sponsors had one month to re-audit trial data on all 1,622 pending applications
Voluntary withdrawal carried no penalty — self-reporting skipped enforcement
Holdouts faced surprise on-site CFDA inspections of sites, hospitals, and CROs
Post-deadline fraud: 3-year filing ban, lost trial licenses, public blacklist

name: Priority Review Pathway
nameZh: 药品优先审评审批程序
agency: China Food and Drug Administration
documentRef: CFDA Announcement No. 230 [2015] (Nov 11, 2015); implementing rules CFDA Announcement No. 19 [2016] (Feb 26, 2016); expanded by CFDA Announcement No. 126 [2017] (Dec 28, 2017)
shortDescription: A fast lane for innovative drugs and rare-disease therapies — review clock cut from 200 working days to 130.
impact: Of 137 oncology drugs approved in China between 2018 and 2021, 98 (72%) went through priority review — the rule that made China-only Phase III trials worth running.
keyProvisions: Five eligible buckets, including drugs not yet sold anywhere in the world
Cuts the standard review clock from 200 working days to 130
Lets the Center for Drug Evaluation talk to sponsors during review
Designations published on a public list, so applicants see what made the cut

name: Marketing Authorization Holder Pilot
nameZh: 药品上市许可持有人制度试点
agency: General Office of the State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: Guo Ban Fa [2016] No. 41
shortDescription: Lets a company own a Chinese drug license without owning the factory — production can be contracted out.
impact: Made asset-light biotech legal in China and seeded the CDMO boom WuXi Biologics rode; by July 2019, 156 holders held 3,239 licenses, and the pilot went national in the 2019 Drug Law.
keyProvisions: Split the drug license from the factory license in 10 pilot provinces
Holder stayed liable end-to-end for quality, recalls, and adverse events
Required product-liability insurance and proven recall capacity
Carved out vaccines, narcotics, blood products, and radioactive drugs

name: China Joins ICH
nameZh: 中国加入国际人用药品注册技术协调会
agency: China Food and Drug Administration (renamed NMPA in 2018)
documentRef: ICH Assembly resolution, Montreal — admitted as Regulatory Member, 1 June 2017
shortDescription: Got China's drug regulator into the global standards body, so reviewers use the same playbook as FDA and EMA.
impact: Foreign trial data now counts toward Chinese approval, so one global Phase 3 covers every market. Innovative-drug IND filings jumped from 688 (2019) to 2,298 (2023).
keyProvisions: Admitted as ICH's 8th Regulatory Member on 1 June 2017, alongside FDA and EMA
CFDA pledged to adopt all ICH guidelines covering GCP, trials, and safety
Foreign trial data became admissible, ending the mandatory-repeat-in-China rule
Won a seat on the ICH Management Committee in 2018, re-elected 2021 and 2024

name: Conditional Approval Pathway
nameZh: 附条件批准上市
agency: General Offices of the CPC Central Committee and the State Council (implemented by CFDA/NMPA; codified by NMPA in 2020)
documentRef: Tingzi [2017] No. 42 — 'Opinions on Deepening the Reform of the Review and Approval System and Encouraging the Innovation of Drugs and Medical Devices'; codified in NMPA Drug Registration Regulation (effective 2020-07-01)
shortDescription: Lets a drug treating a serious unmet need launch on a single Phase 2 trial, with Phase 3 confirmed after launch.
impact: By end-2023 the NMPA had granted 103 conditional approvals, ~85% of them oncology — including the first four domestic PD-1s, all cleared on single-arm Phase 2 data.
keyProvisions: Eligible: life-threatening unmet needs, rare diseases, or pandemic response
Single-arm Phase 2 surrogate data (e.g. response rate) suffices for approval
5-year approval; sponsor must run a confirmatory Phase 3 or face withdrawal
Foreign rare-disease drugs approved abroad can rely on overseas trial data

name: NRDL Annual Negotiations Resume
nameZh: 国家基本医疗保险药品目录
agency: Ministry of Human Resources and Social Security of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: Renshebufa [2017] No. 15 — National Basic Medical Insurance, Work Injury Insurance and Maternity Insurance Drug Catalogue (2017 Edition)
shortDescription: Restarted China's national insurance drug list after an 8-year freeze and made it an annual price negotiation.
impact: Forced the four domestic PD-1 makers to cut prices 64-85% to win national coverage, commoditizing a class the FDA priced at $150K/year.
keyProvisions: Refreshed the catalogue in Feb 2017 (+339 drugs), the first update since 2009
Negotiated 36 high-cost drugs in July 2017 at an average 44% list-price cut
Locked in an annual cycle of price negotiation against a confidential reserve
Listed winners on Tier B at 10-20% co-pay, reimbursable in every province

name: HKEX Chapter 18A
nameZh: 香港主板上市规则第十八A章
agency: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
documentRef: Main Board Listing Rules, Chapter 18A — Biotech Companies
shortDescription: Lets clinical-stage biotechs list in Hong Kong before they have a drug on the market
impact: Solved the valley-of-death financing problem for pre-revenue Chinese biotech; by end-2023, 63 biotechs had listed under the regime, peaking at 20 IPOs in 2021.
keyProvisions: At least one Core Product past the concept stage with regulatory milestones met
Expected market capitalisation ≥ HK$1.5 billion at listing
≥125% working capital for the next 12 months
Meaningful pre-IPO investment from a 'Sophisticated Investor'

name: NHSA Established
nameZh: 国家医疗保障局成立
agency: State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: Plan for Deepening Reform of Party and State Institutions (March 17, 2018) — National Healthcare Security Administration
shortDescription: Merged drug-pricing, health-insurance, and medical-aid powers from four ministries into one national agency.
impact: Gave Beijing one bargaining table for the 1.36 billion people on basic medical insurance, enabling the December 2018 '4+7' pilot that cut 25 generic prices by 52% on average.
keyProvisions: Absorbed public health insurance from the human-resources ministry
Took drug and medical-service price-setting from the NDRC
Absorbed rural medical coverage and medical aid for the poor
Runs the NRDL and nationwide volume-based drug procurement

name: Changsheng Vaccine Scandal
nameZh: 长春长生疫苗事件
agency: Jilin Provincial Food and Drug Administration (initial action) / China Food and Drug Administration
documentRef: Jilin FDA penalty notice (Oct 16, 2018); Changchun Changsheng Bio-Tech Co., Ltd. (SZSE: 002680)
shortDescription: Chinese vaccine maker faked rabies-vaccine records and shipped bad baby DPT shots, forcing Beijing's first vaccine law.
impact: Drew a 9.1B yuan (~$1.3B) fine — China's largest pharma penalty — delisted Changsheng, ousted the CFDA chief, and forced the Vaccine Administration Law in six months.
keyProvisions: Jilin FDA seized 113,000 falsified rabies-vaccine doses on July 15, 2018
9.1B yuan fine — the legal maximum, 30x the bad product's value
252,600 substandard DPT doses had already been given to babies in 2017
Chairwoman Gao Junfang and 13 execs banned from pharma for life

name: He Jiankui Embryo-Editing Case
nameZh: 贺建奎基因编辑婴儿事件
agency: Nanshan District People's Court of Shenzhen (verdict); Standing Committee of the National People's Congress (subsequent legislation)
documentRef: Shenzhen Nanshan Court verdict (2019-12-30); Civil Code Article 1009; Criminal Law Amendment XI, Article 336-1
shortDescription: A Shenzhen scientist used CRISPR to edit three babies' DNA before birth — the world's first — and got 3 years in prison.
impact: Forced the world's first criminal ban on heritable gene editing: up to 7 years for implanting an edited embryo, a flat Civil Code ban, and a new national ethics body over every hospital.
keyProvisions: He got 3 years and a 3M yuan (~US$430k) fine for 'illegal medical practice'
Two collaborators got 18-month and 2-year sentences plus lifetime IVF ban
Crim. Law Amendment 11 (Mar 2021): up to 7 years for implanting edited embryos
Civil Code Art. 1009 (2021): human-gene research must follow law and ethics

name: Volume-Based Procurement
nameZh: 国家组织药品集中采购
agency: National Healthcare Security Administration (with the Joint Procurement Office); pilot plan issued by the General Office of the State Council
documentRef: Guobanfa [2019] No. 2 — Pilot Plan for National Centralized Drug Procurement and Use
shortDescription: Pools every public hospital's drug order into one national auction, with the lowest bidder taking guaranteed volume.
impact: Cut prices on the first 25 drugs by an average of 52% — up to 96% on entecavir — torching the margins that had funded Chinese generics and forcing Hengrui to pivot into innovative drugs.
keyProvisions: Generics must first pass China's 'consistency evaluation' bioequivalence test
Lowest bidder takes the entire guaranteed volume — no second-place award
Pilot cities pre-commit 60–70% of last year's hospital orders to the winner
Launched in 11 cities ('4+7'), went nationwide in 2019, hit insulin by 2021

name: CFDA Renamed NMPA
nameZh: 国家药品监督管理局
agency: State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: Plan for Deepening Reform of Party and State Institutions (March 2018); CFDA English-name notice, 29 August 2018
shortDescription: Renamed China's drug regulator from CFDA to NMPA and folded it into a new market-regulation super-agency.
impact: Locked in the Bi Jingquan reforms — ICH membership, faster reviews, foreign-trial-data acceptance — but cost the regulator a rung of clout, reporting through SAMR instead of the State Council.
keyProvisions: Renamed CFDA to NMPA, English name effective 1 September 2018
Placed NMPA inside SAMR, a new ministerial-level market-regulation super-agency
Dropped the drug regulator from ministerial to vice-ministerial rank
Stripped food safety out; NMPA kept only drugs, devices, and cosmetics

name: Vaccine Administration Law
nameZh: 中华人民共和国疫苗管理法
agency: Standing Committee of the National People's Congress
documentRef: Order No. 30 of the President of the People's Republic of China (2019) — 11th Session of the 13th NPC Standing Committee
shortDescription: Holds vaccine makers legally responsible from R&D through every shot, with criminal penalties for fraud.
impact: Forced industry consolidation and built the regulatory credibility behind WHO emergency-use listings for Sinopharm, Sinovac, and CanSino during the COVID rollout.
keyProvisions: Vaccine licence-holders must own their factory — no contract manufacturers
National e-tracking platform: every vial traced from line to arm, kept 5+ years
Fake vaccines: 15–50× goods-value fine plus licence pulled; substandard: 10–30×
State pays compensation for serious reactions to immunisation-schedule vaccines

name: HGR Regulation Codified
nameZh: 中华人民共和国人类遗传资源管理条例
agency: State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: State Council Order No. 717
shortDescription: Made it illegal to send Chinese DNA samples or genetic data abroad without Beijing's sign-off.
impact: Added a 2-3 month MOST approval step to every multinational China trial before samples could leave, and barred foreign-controlled entities from collecting them at all.
keyProvisions: Foreign firms and China entities they control cannot collect or export HGR
International projects need MOST pre-approval and a Chinese research partner
Any cross-border data transfer requires MOST filing plus a China backup copy
Buying or selling human genetic resources is banned, with fines up to 10x gains

name: STAR Market Launches
nameZh: 科创板
agency: Shanghai Stock Exchange and China Securities Regulatory Commission
documentRef: Rules Governing the Listing of Stocks on the Science and Technology Innovation Board of the Shanghai Stock Exchange (issued 2019-03-01)
shortDescription: Lets loss-making biotechs IPO in mainland China — Beijing's answer to NASDAQ and Hong Kong's 18A.
impact: Gave Chinese biotechs a third home venue; 25 firms raised ~RMB 37B on day one, and by mid-2023 twenty unprofitable biotechs had listed via the biotech-only 'fifth standard' track.
keyProvisions: Switched IPOs to a registration system — market sets price, no regulator veto
Five listing tracks: one needs profits, others use market cap, revenue, or R&D
Biotech track: no profit needed but ≥RMB 4B (~US$560M) cap and a Phase II drug
First mainland board to admit 'red chip' VIEs and weighted-voting-rights firms

name: Biosecurity Law
nameZh: 中华人民共和国生物安全法
agency: Standing Committee of the National People's Congress
documentRef: Biosecurity Law of the People's Republic of China (Presidential Order No. 56) — 10 chapters, 88 articles
shortDescription: China's first single biotech statute — covers pandemics, lab biosafety, gene editing, DNA exports, and bioweapons.
impact: Reframed biotech as national security: high-risk work needs State Council sign-off, foreign access to Chinese pathogen labs and DNA samples is capped, and fines reach RMB 10M or 10–20× unlawful gains.
keyProvisions: Tiers biotech R&D by risk; high-risk work needs State Council sign-off
Bans foreign collection of Chinese human genetic resources without Beijing's OK
Licenses BSL-3/BSL-4 labs; the legal representative is personally liable
Bans bioweapons; implanting a gene-edited embryo is a crime (up to 7 years)

name: Provisions for Drug Registration (2020)
nameZh: 药品注册管理办法
agency: State Administration for Market Regulation
documentRef: SAMR Order No. 27
shortDescription: First overhaul of China's drug-approval rulebook in 13 years, locking the 2015–18 fast-track reforms into permanent law.
impact: Set hard review clocks for the first time — 200 working days standard, 130 priority, 70 for foreign-approved orphan drugs — and became the legal backbone every Chinese biotech now files under.
keyProvisions: Four fast-track lanes: breakthrough, conditional, priority, and emergency review
Standard NDA review capped at 200 working days; priority 130; orphan 70
Clinical trials deemed approved if NMPA stays silent for 60 working days
Lets foreign sponsors and research institutes hold a Chinese drug license

name: Value-Oriented Oncology Guideline
nameZh: 以临床价值为导向的抗肿瘤药物临床研发指导原则
agency: Center for Drug Evaluation, National Medical Products Administration
documentRef: CDE Announcement No. 46 of 2021
shortDescription: Forced Chinese cancer-drug trials to use the best available treatment as the control — not placebo or old chemo.
impact: Closed the me-too lane that had stacked ~80 PD-1 programs behind eight approved checkpoint inhibitors, pushing Akeso, Innovent, and Hengrui into head-to-head trials versus Keytruda.
keyProvisions: Late-stage trials must use the current standard of care as the control
Placebo allowed only when no real treatment exists
Me-too drugs must beat — not just match — the best approved drug
Aimed squarely at the PD-1/PD-L1 and CAR-T pile-up

name: Human Genetic Resources Implementing Rules
nameZh: 人类遗传资源管理条例实施细则
agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: MOST Order No. 21 (科学技术部令第21号), under State Council Order No. 717 (2019 HGR Regulation)
shortDescription: Wrote the operating manual for China's gene-data regime: who needs Beijing's sign-off to touch Chinese DNA.
impact: Locked in that any foreign-controlled entity touching Chinese DNA needs a Chinese partner and MOST sign-off, with fines up to RMB 10M plus 5-10x illegal gains.
keyProvisions: Defined 'HGR information' as gene and genome data only
Raised the population-study approval threshold from 500 to 3,000 cases
Treated foreign-controlled PRC firms (incl. VIEs) as 'foreign parties'
Fines up to RMB 10M plus 5-10x illegal gain; RMB 500K for executives

name: 14th Five-Year Bioeconomy Plan
nameZh: “十四五”生物经济发展规划
agency: National Development and Reform Commission
documentRef: Fa Gai Gao Ji [2021] No. 1850 (发改高技〔2021〕1850号)
shortDescription: China's first national strategy treating biology — not just biopharma — as a pillar of the economy through 2025.
impact: Set a 22-trillion-yuan (~US$3.3T) bioeconomy target by 2025 and signalled to provinces, banks, and state funds that biotech was a pillar industry.
keyProvisions: Names four pillars: bio-medicine, bio-agriculture, bio-manufacturing, bio-info
Sets a 2025 target of growing the bioeconomy to ~22 trillion yuan
Pushes for more biotech firms with annual revenue ≥ RMB 10B — building champions
Designates four regional pilot clusters: BTH, YRD, GBA, Chengdu-Chongqing

name: BIOSECURE Act Introduced
agency: United States Congress (House Select Committee on the CCP and Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee)
shortDescription: US bill to cut off federal contracts, grants, and loans from any company tied to five named Chinese biotechs.
impact: WuXi AppTec stock fell ~30% in two days after the January 2024 introduction; the House passed it 306–81 in September 2024, forcing WuXi to unwind US operations.
keyProvisions: Names BGI, MGI, Complete Genomics, WuXi AppTec, WuXi Bio as 'firms of concern'
Bars federal contracts with the named firms or any vendor using their services
Blocks federal grants and loans — including NIH money — to anyone tied to them
House version gave existing contracts until 2032 to wind down and switch CDMOs

name: BIOSECURE Act Enacted
agency: United States Congress / President of the United States
documentRef: Section 851 of the Servicemember Quality of Life Improvement and National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026 (P.L. 119-60)
shortDescription: Bars US federal agencies from contracting with or funding any firm tied to a Chinese 'biotech of concern'.
impact: Forced WuXi AppTec to sell its US cell-and-gene-therapy units to Altaris and BeiGene to redomicile in Switzerland as BeOne Medicines, all months before the law's first compliance deadline.
keyProvisions: No federal contracts or grants touching a 'biotech of concern' or its tools
BCC list seeded from the Pentagon's 1260H roster, expanded by OMB discretion
First BCC list due Dec 2026; bans kick in 60-90 days after FAR rewrite
Five-year wind-down for existing deals — but not for firms already on 1260H

name: State Council 2025 Reform Guideline
nameZh: 国务院办公厅关于全面深化药品医疗器械监管改革促进医药产业高质量发展的意见
agency: General Office of the State Council of the People's Republic of China
documentRef: Guo Ban Fa [2024] No. 53
shortDescription: Orders China's drug regulator to speed reviews and grant the country's first formal data exclusivity for new drugs.
impact: Cuts innovative-trial review from 60 working days to 30 and post-approval manufacturing reviews from 200 to 60 — the biggest timing shift since 2015 — plus 6-year data exclusivity for new drugs.
keyProvisions: Innovative-drug clinical-trial review cut from 60 working days to 30
Post-approval manufacturing-change reviews cut from 200 days to 60
First formal data exclusivity: 6 years for new drugs, 3 for improved
Rare-disease drugs approvable on foreign trial data, skipping China bridge
